excerpt: That said, the organizational challenges are often harder to solve than the technical ones. In practice, when multiple teams or domains…

content.html: You're right that shared databases aren't inherently an anti-pattern—and with proper governance (e.g., treating schema as a versioned API, using read replicas, etc.), many technical risks can be mitigated.
That said, the organizational challenges are often harder to solve than the technical ones. In practice, when multiple teams or domains share a single schema, ownership ambiguity leads to:
Uncoordinated schema changes (“just a small ALTER” that breaks another team’s assumptions),
Slower evolution due to cross-team coordination overhead,
Blurred accountability during incidents or data quality issues.

Even with good intent, Conway’s Law tends to surface: if services are independent, but data isn’t, coupling sneaks in at the persistence layer—undermining many of the benefits you hoped to gain from separation.

Microservices aren’t always the answer (and premature decomposition does add complexity), but bounded contexts with clear data ownership—even if initially co-located in one DB instance—help avoid the “shared schema trap.” Physical separation can then follow incrementally, when the domain boundaries are stable.
So yes—it’s situational. But in my experience, investing early in logical data boundaries pays dividends, even if physical isolation comes later.
